quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Arrhenius acid/base"?

options.text: Loss of electrons; occurs at the anode; oxidation state increases
Acid: produces H⁺(aq); base: produces OH⁻(aq); limited to aqueous solutions
An unwanted reaction that competes with the desired reaction, consuming reactants and reducing the yield of the target product
A chemical equation with equal numbers of each atom on both sides, satisfying conservation of mass

quiz_questions.explanation: "Arrhenius acid/base" is defined as: Acid: produces H⁺(aq); base: produces OH⁻(aq); limited to aqueous solutions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Brønsted-Lowry acid/base"?

options.text: Gas particles are in constant random motion, collisions are elastic, average KE is proportional to absolute temperature (KE = ³⁄₂kT)
Equilibrium involving species in more than one phase; pure solids and liquids have activity = 1 and are excluded from K
Acid: proton (H⁺) donor; base: proton acceptor; broader than Arrhenius, applicable in non-aqueous solutions
Concentration in moles of solute per liter of solution (mol/L); the most common concentration unit in chemistry

quiz_questions.explanation: "Brønsted-Lowry acid/base" is defined as: Acid: proton (H⁺) donor; base: proton acceptor; broader than Arrhenius, applicable in non-aqueous solutions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Strong vs. weak acid"?

options.text: The amount of product actually obtained from a reaction; measured experimentally
Relates free energy to cell potential; n = moles of electrons transferred, F = Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol)
A proton (H⁺) acceptor
Strong acids dissociate 100% (Ka very large); weak acids partially dissociate (Ka < 1, typically 10⁻² to 10⁻¹⁰)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Strong vs. weak acid" is defined as: Strong acids dissociate 100% (Ka very large); weak acids partially dissociate (Ka < 1, typically 10⁻² to 10⁻¹⁰). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Amphoteric substance"?

options.text: Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy, from lowest to highest
A substance that can act as either an acid or a base (e.g., water: H₂O + H₂O ⇌ H₃O⁺ + OH⁻; Al(OH)₃)
The ratio of product to reactant concentrations at equilibrium, each raised to their stoichiometric coefficients; temperature-dependent
The point where the indicator changes color; ideally matches the equivalence point; slightly different in practice

quiz_questions.explanation: "Amphoteric substance" is defined as: A substance that can act as either an acid or a base (e.g., water: H₂O + H₂O ⇌ H₃O⁺ + OH⁻; Al(OH)₃). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Acid-Base Definitions, which best defines "Lewis acid/base"?

options.text: An acid that can donate more than one proton: diprotic (H₂SO₄, H₂CO₃) or triprotic (H₃PO₄)
ΔTf = iKfm; solute particles lower the freezing point by disrupting crystal formation
The mass of one mole of a substance in grams per mole (g/mol); numerically equal to the atomic/molecular mass in amu
Acid: electron pair acceptor; base: electron pair donor; the broadest definition, encompassing reactions without proton transfer

quiz_questions.explanation: In Acid-Base Definitions, "Lewis acid/base" refers to: Acid: electron pair acceptor; base: electron pair donor; the broadest definition, encompassing reactions without proton transfer.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Acid: produces H⁺(aq); base: produces OH⁻(aq); limited to aqueous solutions"?

options.text: Kw (autoionization constant)
Arrhenius acid/base
Lewis acid/base
Nernst equation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Arrhenius acid/base".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Acid: proton (H⁺) donor; base: proton acceptor; broader than Arrhenius, applicable in non-aqueous solutions"?

options.text: ΔG° = -nFE°
Catalyst effect
Brønsted-Lowry acid/base
ΔS prediction rules

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Brønsted-Lowry acid/base".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Strong acids dissociate 100% (Ka very large); weak acids partially dissociate (Ka < 1, typically 10⁻² to 10⁻¹⁰)"?

options.text: Crystalline solid
Strong vs. weak acid
Intermediate
Formal charge

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Strong vs. weak acid".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A substance that can act as either an acid or a base (e.g., water: H₂O + H₂O ⇌ H₃O⁺ + OH⁻; Al(OH)₃)"?

options.text: Factors affecting rate
Integrated rate law
Amphoteric substance
pOH

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Amphoteric substance".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Acid-Base Definitions, which term matches: "Acid: electron pair acceptor; base: electron pair donor; the broadest definition, encompassing reactions without proton transfer"?

options.text: Activation energy (Ea)
Lewis acid/base
Kinetic molecular theory
Homogeneous catalyst

quiz_questions.explanation: In Acid-Base Definitions, this refers to "Lewis acid/base".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Acid-Base Definitions is accurate?

options.text: Acid-Base Definitions have evolved from Arrhenius (acids produce H⁺, bases produce OH⁻ in water) to Brønsted-Lowry (acids donate H⁺, bases accept H⁺) to Lewis (acids accept electron pairs, bases donate electron pairs).
K > 1 favors products; K < 1 favors reactants.
The 5% approximation simplifies calculations when [HA]₀/Ka > 400.
Solids and Liquids exhibit distinct properties based on their intermolecular forces and particle arrangements.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Acid-Base Definitions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Acid-Base Definitions?

options.text: The Brønsted-Lowry definition is most commonly used in AP Chemistry.
K is dimensionless and temperature-dependent.
K > 1 favors products; K < 1 favors reactants.
Zero-order: rate is independent of concentration; first-order: rate doubles when concentration doubles; second-order: rate quadruples when concentration doubles.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Acid-Base Definitions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Acid-Base Definitions?

options.text: Batteries and Corrosion applies electrochemistry to practical applications.
For aA + bB → products, rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ, where m and n are reaction orders (determined experimentally, NOT from coefficients).
Strong acids (HCl, HBr, HI, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, HClO₄) and strong bases (group 1/2 hydroxides) dissociate completely.
Solids and Liquids exhibit distinct properties based on their intermolecular forces and particle arrangements.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Acid-Base Definitions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Acid-Base Definitions?

options.text: Calorimetry measures ΔH experimentally: in coffee-cup calorimetry (constant pressure), q = mcΔT; in bomb calorimetry (constant volume), q = CcalΔT.
Weak acids/bases partially dissociate with Ka/Kb values <1.
Colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure, vapor pressure lowering) depend on solute particle concentration, not identity.
The relative strength of an acid is inversely related to the strength of its conjugate base.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Acid-Base Definitions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Acid-Base Definitions?

options.text: Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles forms the foundation of chemistry.

Acid-Base Definitions have evolved from Arrhenius (acids produce H⁺, bases produce OH⁻ in water) to Brønsted-Lowry (acids donate H⁺, bases accept H⁺) to Lewis (acids accept electron pairs, bases donate electron pairs).
Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) has a strong first dissociation and weak second (Ka2 = 0.012).
The atomic number (Z) equals the number of protons and defines the element.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Acid-Base Definitions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Acid-Base Definitions?

options.text: Household acids and bases: vinegar (acetic acid, Ka = 1.8×10⁻⁵), lemon juice (citric acid), baking soda (NaHCO₃ acts as a weak base)
Forensic chemistry using stoichiometric analysis to determine the composition of unknown substances from mass spectral data
Mass spectrometry separating isotopes by mass-to-charge ratio to determine elemental composition in forensics and pharmaceutical analysis
Industrial ammonia production optimizing the ratio of N₂ to H₂ to minimize waste and maximize yield in the Haber process

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Acid-Base Definitions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Acid-Base Definitions?

options.text: Aluminum's amphoteric nature: Al(OH)₃ dissolves in both acids and bases, exploited in the Bayer process to purify alumina from bauxite ore
Rocket fuel calculations: precise stoichiometry of liquid H₂ and O₂ (2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O) ensures maximum thrust without wasting expensive fuel
Kidney stone formation: when [Ca²⁺][C₂O₄²⁻] exceeds Ksp of calcium oxalate (2.3 × 10⁻⁹), crystals precipitate in the kidney
Catalytic converters using Pt, Pd, and Rh heterogeneous catalysts to lower Ea for converting exhaust pollutants (CO, NOₓ) to harmless products

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Acid-Base Definitions.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Acid-Base Definitions applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Kidney dialysis using osmotic pressure principles to remove waste from blood across a semipermeable membrane
Lewis acid catalysis in organic synthesis: BF₃ and AlCl₃ as Lewis acids in Friedel-Crafts reactions for pharmaceutical manufacturing
Hand warmers using exothermic iron oxidation (4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃) to release heat on demand for hours
Carbon dioxide equilibrium in oceans (CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺) affected by rising atmospheric CO₂, causing ocean acidification

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Acid-Base Definitions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Acid-Base Definitions?

options.text: Household acids and bases: vinegar (acetic acid, Ka = 1.8×10⁻⁵), lemon juice (citric acid), baking soda (NaHCO₃ acts as a weak base)
Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent
pH meters measuring voltage differences between a glass electrode and reference electrode, converted to pH via the Nernst equation
Industrial process design using Hess's Law to predict energy requirements for multi-step chemical syntheses without performing every reaction experimentally

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Acid-Base Definitions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Acid-Base Definitions in practice?

options.text: Aluminum's amphoteric nature: Al(OH)₃ dissolves in both acids and bases, exploited in the Bayer process to purify alumina from bauxite ore
Radiocarbon dating using the ¹⁴C isotope to determine the age of organic materials up to ~50,000 years old
Haber process optimization (N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃) using high pressure and moderate temperature to shift equilibrium toward ammonia production for fertilizers
Chain reaction mechanisms in combustion: initiation produces radicals, propagation sustains the reaction, termination removes radicals (relevant to engine knocking and explosives)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Acid-Base Definitions.
